danger_signals.signal: Environmental hazards in Death Match stages
danger_signals.visual_form: Stage-specific environmental obstacles and interactive elements
danger_signals.meaning: Danger zones that threaten both players; world-state of heightened combat risk

danger_signals.visual_form: Visually distinct final opponents with enhanced/altered designs
danger_signals.meaning: Escalating threat level; these enemies represent existential danger to tournament progression

danger_signals.signal: Seesaw lifebar mechanic in Death Match
danger_signals.visual_form: Dynamic lifebar with center equilibrium point that shifts with damage
danger_signals.meaning: Visual communication of unstable, precarious combat state; danger of sudden defeat if arrow reaches opponent's side

safety_signals.visual_form: Standard arena layouts without environmental hazards
safety_signals.meaning: Controlled combat environment; danger is limited to direct opponent threat

safety_signals.visual_form: Visually distinct 14 playable characters with varied designs
safety_signals.meaning: Multiple viable strategic paths; players have agency and options for safe playstyle selection

faction_indicators.faction: Original World Heroes fighters (8 characters from first game)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Returning character designs with modified/updated move sets
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Selected randomly in tournament progression after defeating all 6 new fighters

faction_indicators.faction: New World Heroes 2 fighters (6 new characters)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Fresh character archetypes introduced to expand fighter diversity
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Must be defeated first in Normal Game mode tournament bracket

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Visually enhanced/corrupted versions of antagonist characters
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Final tournament opponents; represent external threats to Dr. Brown's tournament legitimacy

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Standard stage environments with balanced arena layouts
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Characters fight within conventional tournament rules; standard lifebar mechanics apply

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Stages feature environmental hazards; seesaw lifebar replaces standard health tracking
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Combat becomes unpredictable; environmental threats force tactical adaptation; standing up mechanic after knockdown adds survival pressure

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Neo-Geegus and Dio boss characters appear; corrupted visual design suggests otherworldly threat
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: These opponents disrupt normal tournament flow; represent narrative conflict from first game's unresolved interference

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Player advances through fighter roster tiers; defeated opponents removed from battlefield
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Progressive tournament advancement; player demonstrates growing mastery through sequential victories

environmental_storytelling.technique: Tournament bracket structure as narrative progression
environmental_storytelling.example: Player must defeat 6 new fighters first, then 4 randomly-selected originals, finally 2 bosses—visual/mechanical progression communicates escalating difficulty and narrative stakes

environmental_storytelling.technique: Death Match stage design as world-state indicator
environmental_storytelling.example: Hazardous environments in Death Match mode contrast with safe Normal Game arenas, suggesting Dr. Brown's increased desperation to resolve the tournament conclusively

environmental_storytelling.technique: Character visual design as historical/cultural identity
environmental_storytelling.example: 14 distinct fighter designs reference historical/mythological figures (Jeanne D'Arc, Hanzou Hattori, Rasputin, etc.); visual archetypes communicate each fighter's origin and thematic identity without explicit text
Seesaw lifebar in Death Match vs. standard lifebar in Normal Game—mechanical difference reinforces narrative shift toward instability and desperation

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Standard attack chains, projectile moves, counter-grabs; predictable move sets within competitive fighting game conventions
Same moves available but environmental hazards force repositioning; knockdown standing mechanic adds survival behavior
Enhanced combat difficulty; visually corrupted design suggests altered/dangerous capabilities compared to standard fighters
Ultimate tournament threat; final obstacle representing maximum difficulty and narrative climax
4 of 8 original fighters appear at random after first 6 new fighters defeated—communicates uncertainty and unpredictability in tournament flow

notes: World Heroes 2 communicates world-state primarily through mechanical and structural means rather than explicit visual theming. The game lacks documented faction color identities or symbolic markers—instead, it uses character design diversity, stage hazards, and lifebar mechanics to signal danger and tournament progression. The Death Match mode represents an escalated world-state where Dr. Brown's desperation manifests mechanically through environmental hazards and the seesaw lifebar system. The narrative of Geegus's prior interference is reinforced by Neo-Geegus and Dio appearing as boss opponents, visually distinct from standard fighters. Danger signals are primarily mechanical (knockdown standing, environmental threats, boss encounters) rather than environmental color-coding.
